title: Powering the data boom: answering nine key questions about powering AI data centres
gist: The biggest constraint on AI data centre buildout is no longer compute but grid electricity access; Wood Mackenzie's Chris Seiple explains developers are turning to interruptible grid connections and behind-the-meter generation as bridge solutions, even though the regulatory rules governing them are still being written.

themes.theme: Power, not compute, is the binding constraint on AI data centres

insights.claim: The biggest constraint facing AI infrastructure is no longer demand for computing power but access to reliable electricity, as hyperscalers race to secure power in northern Virginia, Texas and other parts of the US.
insights.speaker: Chris Seiple
insights.so_what: Investors evaluating AI infrastructure buildout timelines should model grid interconnection speed, not chip supply, as the primary bottleneck to capacity additions.
insights.quote: The biggest constraint facing AI infrastructure is no longer demand for computing power - it's access to reliable electricity.

insights.claim: Transmission limitations and lagging interconnection processes are progressing far more slowly than development timelines for data centres that now sometimes exceed 1 GW in size.
insights.speaker: Chris Seiple
insights.so_what: Data centre projects at gigawatt scale face structural grid-timeline mismatches; expect continued delays or unconventional power solutions for large hyperscale campuses.

themes.theme: Unconventional bridge solutions gaining mainstream traction

insights.claim: Data centre developers facing grid constraints are pursuing three broad approaches: delaying start-up until the grid catches up, accepting unreliable/interruptible grid connections to allow early operation, or building on-site (behind-the-meter) generation as a bridge until permanent grid connections arrive.
insights.speaker: Chris Seiple
insights.so_what: Provides investors a framework to classify data centre projects by power strategy and assess relative execution risk and timeline for each approach.

insights.claim: Interruptible grid connections and behind-the-meter generation/storage solutions have rapidly moved from niche concepts to mainstream solutions for bringing data centres online years before grids could otherwise deliver power.
insights.speaker: Chris Seiple
insights.so_what: Signals a fast-growing addressable market for on-site/BTM generation, gas turbines, and storage equipment suppliers serving hyperscale data centre campuses.
insights.quote: The bridge period is creating unprecedented demand for alternative options, which have rapidly moved from niche concepts to mainstream solutions.

insights.claim: Interruptible grid connections and behind-the-meter solutions pose significant technical, commercial and regulatory risks that are often underestimated by data centre developers.
insights.speaker: Chris Seiple
insights.so_what: A caution flag against assuming BTM/interruptible power is a low-risk shortcut; investors should scrutinize regulatory and commercial exposure in projects relying on these structures.
insights.quote: Yet these approaches also pose significant technical, commercial and regulatory risks that are often underestimated.

insights.claim: Regulations governing interruptible grid connections and behind-the-meter power models are still being developed by regional transmission organisations (RTOs), utilities and regulators, meaning developers are making billion-dollar investment decisions before the operating rules are finalized.
insights.speaker: Chris Seiple
insights.so_what: Regulatory uncertainty is a material tail risk for capital already committed to BTM/interruptible data centre power projects; investors should monitor RTO rulemakings as a catalyst.
insights.quote: Developers are making billion dollar investment decisions while some of the operating rules that will ultimately govern their projects are still evolving.
